workorders.explanation: Hyperbole is a deliberate exaggeration used for emphasis or humour, not meant to be taken literally. 'I'm so hungry I could eat a horse' or 'This bag weighs a tonne' are hyperbole — they exaggerate to make a point. ✓

workorders.correctText: No — 'hard' means with great effort; 'hardly' means almost not at all
currentOptions: Yes, they mean exactly the same
No — 'hard' means with great effort; 'hardly' means almost not at all
Yes, 'hardly' is just a more formal version
No — 'hard' is an adjective; 'hardly' is a noun

distractorTargets.currentText: Yes, they mean exactly the same
Yes, 'hardly' is just a more formal version
No — 'hard' is an adjective; 'hardly' is a noun

workorders.explanation: They mean very different things! 'She works hard' = she puts in lots of effort. 'She hardly works' = she barely does any work at all. Adding '-ly' to 'hard' completely changes the meaning — one of English's sneakiest traps! ✓

workorders.question: In 'I stayed inside because it was raining', which part is the subordinate clause?
workorders.correctText: because it was raining
workorders.explanation: 'Because it was raining' is the subordinate clause — it starts with 'because' (a subordinating conjunction) and can't stand alone as a sentence. 'I stayed inside' is the main clause — it makes sense on its own. ✓

workorders.question: Which sentence has TWO clauses?
workorders.correctText: I went home because I felt ill.
currentOptions: I went home because I felt ill.
workorders.explanation: 'I went home because I felt ill' has two clauses: 'I went home' (main clause) and 'because I felt ill' (subordinate clause). The others all have just one clause each. ✓

workorders.question: What is the difference between a main clause and a subordinate clause?
workorders.correctText: Main clauses make sense alone; subordinate clauses don't
currentOptions: Main clauses make sense alone; subordinate clauses don't
Main clauses have adjectives; subordinate clauses don't

distractorTargets.currentText: Main clauses have adjectives; subordinate clauses don't

workorders.explanation: A main clause makes complete sense on its own ('The dog barked'). A subordinate clause depends on the main clause — it can't stand alone ('because it heard a noise'). Together: 'The dog barked because it heard a noise.' ✓

workorders.question: In 'Although she practised daily, she still lost the match', which is the main clause?
workorders.correctText: she still lost the match
workorders.explanation: 'She still lost the match' is the main clause — it makes sense on its own. 'Although she practised daily' is the subordinate clause (starts with 'although'). The subordinate clause comes first here, but the main clause is still the independent one. ✓

workorders.question: Why is 'the' classified as a determiner rather than an adjective?
workorders.correctText: Because it identifies which noun, not what it's like
currentOptions: Because it identifies which noun, not what it's like
Because it comes after the noun

distractorTargets.currentText: Because it comes after the noun

workorders.explanation: 'The' identifies WHICH noun you mean (the specific one), but it doesn't describe what the noun is LIKE. Adjectives describe qualities (big, red, happy). Determiners specify identity (the, a, this, my, every). Different jobs! ✓

workorders.question: In 'The record was broken yesterday', is 'broken' a verb or adjective?
workorders.correctText: Verb — it's the past participle of 'break'
currentOptions: Verb — it's the past participle of 'break'
Both interpretations are valid depending on reading

distractorTargets.currentText: Both interpretations are valid depending on reading

workorders.explanation: 'Broken' is a VERB here — part of the passive voice ('was broken' = someone broke it). In 'a broken window', it would be an adjective (describing the window's state). The passive voice uses 'was/were + past participle'. ✓
